records.ety: [F. retirer; pref. re- re- + tirer to draw. See Tirade.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Retired (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Retiring.]

defs: To withdraw; to take away; -- sometimes used reflexively.
quotes: He . . . retired himself, his wife, and children into a forest.
As when the sun is present all the year, And never doth retire his golden ray.

defs: To withdraw from circulation, or from the market; to take up and pay; as, to retire bonds; to retire a note.
To cause to retire; specifically, to designate as no longer qualified for active service; to place on the retired list; as, to retire a military or naval officer.
To go back or return; to draw back or away; to keep aloof; to withdraw or retreat, as from observation; to go into privacy; as, to retire to his home; to retire from the world, or from notice.
quotes: To Una back he cast him to retire.
The mind contracts herself, and shrinketh in, And to herself she gladly doth retire.

defs: To retreat from action or danger; to withdraw for safety or pleasure; as, to retire from battle.
quotes: Set Uriah in the forefront of the hottest battle, and retire ye from him, that he may be smitten, and die.

defs: To withdraw from a public station, or from business; as, having made a large fortune, he retired.
quotes: And from Britannia's public posts retire.

defs: To recede; to fall or bend back; as, the shore of the sea retires in bays and gulfs.
To go to bed; as, he usually retires early.
The act of retiring, or the state of being retired; also, a place to which one retires.
quotes: The battle and the retire of the English succors.
[Eve] discover'd soon the place of her retire.

defs: A call sounded on a bugle, announcing to skirmishers that they are to retire, or fall back.
